page.content: The data presented in the table offers a comprehensive overview of the physical capability of children in performing sit-ups without assistance, segmented by age groups. Specifically, the columns labeled “≤ 15.0 years of age” and “> 15.0 years of age” provide insight into the percentage of children capable of executing this fundamental exercise within these distinct age brackets. Notably, the data indicates that 68.6% of children aged 15.0 years or younger can perform sit-ups independently, compared to 71.1% in those older than 15.0 years. Additionally, the table includes genetic information, such as the SNP rs1790858, with corresponding metrics like minor allele frequency (MAF) and odds ratios, which may suggest a correlation between genetic factors and physical fitness capabilities.

page.content: These findings underscore the importance of considering both developmental stages and potential genetic influences when assessing physical fitness levels in youth populations. Such insights could inform targeted interventions aimed at enhancing physical activity and overall health in children and adolescents.

lines.text: These findings underscore the importance of considering both developmental stages and potential genetic influences when
assessing physical fitness levels in youth populations. Such insights could inform targeted interventions aimed at enhancing
physical activity and overall health in children and adolescents.
